title: How public opinion is turning against AI
excerpt: Journalist Jasmine Sun examines the origins of anti-AI protests in popular skepticism of the technology as an elite project.
content_html:
AI was supposed to make our lives better. Instead, it’s made many of us scared and angry. Communities are protesting against the building of new data centers — the warehouses of IT equipment powering the AI buildout — across the country, and increasingly they’re winning. And polling shows most Americans think AI is moving too fast.

So how did public opinion on AI curdle so quickly? Jasmine Sun, who reports on the industry from San Francisco, argues that the backlash treats AI less as a technology and more as a political project. “The debate was not about like, is ChatGPT useful to me?” Sun told me during a taping of Vox’s The Gray Area. “The debate was actually something more like, there are these big corporations and unaccountable billionaires…coming into my city, coming into my life and changing it without having any sort of democratic input?”

Filling in for Sean Illing, I talked to Sun about the rise of “AI populism,” the parallels with the Industrial Revolution, and how the backlash could crash into the 2028 presidential election.

As always, there’s much more in the full podcast, which drops every Monday, so listen to and follow us on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Pandora, or wherever you find podcasts.

You’ve been writing about a phenomenon you call AI populism. How would you define that? What is AI populism?

I define AI populism as a worldview where AI is not seen as an ordinary technology, but specifically as an elite political project to be resisted. I came to the term while thinking about the AI backlash and the reasons people are increasingly anti-AI — whether that’s LLM slop, whether that’s Waymos in their city, whether that’s a new data center project. One thing that occurred to me was that a lot of times the debate wasn’t about whether ChatGPT is useful to me, or whether Waymos are safer than a human driver. The debate was actually something more like: There are these big corporations and unaccountable billionaires who are coming into my city, coming into my life, and changing it without any democratic input.

When I talk to people who are opposing AI in various ways, they seem more concerned with this concentration-of-power, anti-elite dimension — which is where I take the word “populism” — rather than classic AI safety concerns, which are more about the technical characteristics that might introduce risk.

You wrote a piece that touched on some of this but went to a darker place — “AI populism’s warning shots” — and you wrote about actual shots. Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, was targeted by a Molotov cocktail and a shooting within the span of a couple of days. There was an Indiana councilman who voted for a data center and woke up to gunshots at his home and a note reading “no data centers.” Why do you think of those incidents of violence as warning shots of something to come?

It was pretty scary. I’m no Sam Altman fanboy, but it’s terrifying that assassination attempts are showing up in response to people’s worries about AI. One factor is that we’ve been seeing a rising wave of political violence and support for political violence in the US, especially among young people, over the past few years — the UnitedHealthcare CEO shooting, the Charlie Kirk shooting. Increasingly, a lot of disaffected, maybe nihilistic young people are turning toward political violence as a way to express political beliefs they don’t feel they have other channels for. Or maybe that person is just unwell. But I do expect to see more of it, because my theory of political discontent is that if people feel they have institutional channels to bargain for their rights — if they feel the democratic process is working, or they’re part of a union and believe their union leader will go bargain about how automation shows up in the workplace — they’ll most likely go through those channels.

When it feels like the official channels aren’t working, opposition becomes much more diffuse and volatile. That’s part of why, in creative communities, you’ll see people witch-hunting each other over AI use. I think we’ll see more political violence against people seen as AI leaders, or as supporting AI leaders.

That’s really scary.

Yeah, I’m quite worried about it. But again, my sense is that it comes from a feeling of — what else is there to be done, when you have this level of concentration of wealth and power, and there’s no democratic input right now into how AI is regulated or built?

It’s like a jump straight from complaining at your community meeting about the data center to an act of violence.

I was talking to some friends about this. During the 20th century in the US, there was a wave of factory mechanization and automation, but unions were really strong — often when a company said, “We’re going to bring in these machines,” they’d sit down with the factory union leader and say, “Okay, you can bring in the machines, but we’re going to couple that with a wage increase,” or a 35-hour workweek, or earlier retirement. There was a channel to make a deal about how automation would show up in your workplace. That meant people were more likely to accept it as something lifting all boats. I don’t think that’s happening now — most of the industries affected by AI aren’t organized in labor unions, and the democratic channels are questionable at best.

It’s like when people have agency to be part of the transition, the process goes a lot smoother. Is there a historical analogy for a technological change that didn’t allow for input from the people involved? I’m thinking of the Luddites.

The Luddites are a good example. When the automated looms were introduced, there was a lot of violence against the looms. The book I’d really recommend here is Carl Benedikt Frey’s The Technology Trap. He’s an Oxford economist who studied a ton of historical examples — in Europe, in China, all over the world — including the Luddites and 20th-century automation. His central question was: In what contexts do workers successfully stop automation, and in what contexts do they allow it to be introduced? How does the political environment, or the balance of power between people and their leaders, change the outcome? He found that when automation was introduced alongside social welfare policies — a higher minimum wage, some form of redistribution — people were much more willing to accept it, which is fairly rational.

I want to talk about Silicon Valley’s understanding of this backlash more generally. You’re painting a pretty dark picture, and you’re right in the belly of the beast in San Francisco — I’m sure you talk to people involved with AI every day. Is there a moment when it clicked for them that this backlash is real and something they have to take seriously? Or has that happened yet?

I’ve definitely noticed a huge difference, over the past six months, in how seriously people in Silicon Valley take the AI backlash.

Like what?

People just talk about it more. I’d bring up AI populism to people last year, and they’d normally say, “It doesn’t matter — technology always introduces some discontent, people get annoyed but they get used to it, like the internet.” That was the standard reaction last year. Not anymore. I think part of the reason OpenAI and Anthropic have felt pressure to introduce economic policy proposals around job automation is that they’re seeing how worried people are. The data center moratoriums and the broader data center backlash have been surprising and meaningful in getting AI leaders to recognize they have both a messaging problem and an actual problem with the product and the technology they’re introducing.

A lot of the increasing opposition to AI in Washington has caused people to see this too. At first, Trump — as you mentioned — was very pro-AI. He and David Sacks were accelerationists; they wanted AI to go faster and to block attempts at regulation.

He was the AI czar.

“The moratoriums, the regulation fights, even the booing at graduations, the literal assassination attempts — people in Silicon Valley have become much more worried.”

He was the AI czar — he’s no longer the AI czar. But it turned out a lot of other constituencies, both on the left and the right, were pretty opposed. For example, Trump and David Sacks tried to introduce a big federal bill that would preempt all state-level AI regulation — no state could regulate AI for 10 years. They tried to sneak it into a big omnibus bill so no one would notice. But members of Congress realized it was happening, and — whether for kid-safety reasons or frontier-safety reasons — people said, Wait a second, the idea of preventing any state from regulating AI for ten years is crazy. A lot of people organized in Washington to successfully stop that preemption. I think that showed the scale and bipartisanship of a coalition that was very keen to make sure it stayed possible to regulate AI was underestimated. As a result of all this — the moratoriums, the regulation fights, even the booing at graduations, the literal assassination attempts — people in Silicon Valley have become much more worried.

China is our big competitor in the AI race, and it certainly has all the conditions for a populist pushback to AI — youth unemployment is really high, and AI technology is in some ways more advanced at taking over real-world jobs. I was watching a video about fully automated factories and a robot pharmacist. You’re one of the rare American tech reporters who gets to spend time in China, and you wrote a piece that surprised me — you found there wasn’t really a populist backlash to AI there. Why not?

I was really interested in this question, and I was finishing my New York Times piece while in China for a few weeks, talking to both AI people and non-AI people. The main reason there’s not a big populist backlash in China is that there isn’t a lot of social unrest or populist backlash against anything — the entire MO of the Chinese government, the No. 1 priority, is domestic social stability. Any whisper of protest gets shut down; that’s why they have such strong speech controls. So one factor is that China doesn’t have much of a culture of resistance in general, whether in workplaces or politically. I’m not saying no one dissents — but it has a cultural effect too, because people don’t see it as useful or as an option. When I ask family members of mine in China about AI, sometimes they’re annoyed about specific things, but fundamentally, the idea of opposing AI is seen as almost unimaginable.

The other thing about China is that if you’re middle-aged there, you’ve lived through so many political, economic, and technological revolutions in your lifetime. When I was a little kid visiting Shanghai in the mid-2000s, there were no high-speed trains — now China has some of the best high-speed rail systems in the world. Technology has always gone hand in hand with dramatic economic advancement, with being lifted out of poverty. The modernization process has been aggressive and disruptive, but it’s not something the party has offered opportunities to resist, and it’s something most Chinese people still see as an inevitability that was mostly good for most people — because incomes did increase by dramatic amounts alongside the technological change. So I think people have a similar attitude toward AI: It’s much less about “Can I stop the AI wave?” and more “How can I take advantage of the AI wave to get ahead economically?”

We were just talking about this deep pessimism about what technology can bring us here in the US. I think a lot of people look around and think: We don’t have a cure for cancer yet, but we’ve sure seen our lives get worse in a lot of ways because of technology, social media, whatever. That pessimism probably fuels the backlash to AI, the skepticism about whether it can ever deliver on its promises. And that experience just isn’t the same in China, or probably much of the rest of the world, where technological progress has been faster and more concrete in people’s lives.

My 90-year-old grandfather said he’d love an elder-care robot to help him do tasks around the house so he doesn’t have to rely on his kids — he wants more freedom and mobility. It’s seen more as a tool to help individual goals. Even with the robot factories or pharmacies — one thing that struck me visiting a robot pharmacy was that the PR people happily said, “Yep, we’re doing these robots because human workers take too many smoke breaks and bathroom breaks and take too long.”

You’d never say that in the US, but they’re probably thinking the same thing — they just don’t say it. The other thing they mentioned is that this lets the pharmacy operate 24/7, because a lot of people need medications in the middle of the night and want to order via the DoorDash equivalent. There was actually a labor shortage before — Chinese workers weren’t willing to work night shifts — so these pharmacies are offering real consumer surplus. A significant percentage of orders come in overnight, when no other pharmacy is open. And with the factories, part of the issue is that Chinese workers, especially young people, don’t want to do factory work anymore.

“I think the 2028 presidential primary and election is really where I expect AI to become a centerpiece of the conversation.”

That anecdote gets at the promise and peril of AI, and the role of the backlash movement — which I’m still wrestling with how I feel about. On the one hand, I want to live in a world where cancer gets cured, where we live in an era of abundance, where things are cheap and easy to make because factories can run all the time with machine workers who don’t require anything — I want the future we were promised, of flying cars and everything working well.

But I also don’t want to lose my job, or see humanity wiped out by an angry machine god. Because we don’t really know what’s going to happen yet, it’s hard to work out my own feelings about the pushback here in the States — what’s appropriate, and what’s holding us back from real advances in our lives.

Totally, I agree. I like Waymos — I think they’re safer, and I’d prefer a safer robot car driv[ing] me around instead of me driving. I’m not a good driver; no one should let me drive. So I wrestle with some of the same things.

To close out the conversation — let’s come back to the United States. AI populism is brewing as a political force. We’ve seen it show up in a couple of races so far, but it’s early. We’ve got the midterms, then the presidential election. How do you think it’s going to affect American politics this November, and in 2028?

My sense is that this November, it’s going to be more about state and local races where AI really shows up. I’m going to spend some time in Michigan and Wisconsin this summer touring some of the data center sites facing the most opposition — those states also have contested governor and Senate races where AI and data centers have become a core issue, so I’m interested to learn more there. I think the 2028 presidential primary and election is really where I expect AI to become a centerpiece of the conversation — especially if we start to see some of the employment impacts people are expecting. As soon as we see something like a 2 percent rise in unemployment, if that happens, I think people will be very upset, and we should expect a ton of focus on the issue.

The other thing I’ll note is political opportunism — you’re already seeing a bit of this, where politicians are likely to raise the salience of AI above where people might ordinarily care about it, because it’s become a convenient boogeyman. It polls so poorly, people are so anti-AI and anti-data-center, AI billionaires are so unsympathetic, that no matter what your policy program is, AI is a great reason to push it. I think a lot of politicians who are being clever about this are going to move AI to the center of the conversation, raising its salience to manufacture urgency for proposals they’re already excited about. That’s definitely something I’m watching for 2028.

sentences.zh: “这场辩论实际上更像是，这些大公司和不负责任的亿万富翁……进入我的城市，进入我的生活并在没有任何民主投入的情况下改变它？”

blocks.html_en: Filling in for Sean Illing, I talked to Sun about the rise of “AI populism,” the parallels with the Industrial Revolution, and how the backlash could crash into the 2028 presidential election.

sentences.en: Filling in for Sean Illing, I talked to Sun about the rise of “AI populism,” the parallels with the Industrial Revolution, and how the backlash could crash into the 2028 presidential election.
sentences.zh: 我代替 Sean Illing 与 Sun 讨论了“人工智能民粹主义”的兴起、与工业革命的相似之处，以及这种强烈反对可能如何影响 2028 年总统选举。

blocks.html_en: As always, there’s much more in the full podcast, which drops every Monday, so listen to and follow us on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Pandora, or wherever you find podcasts.

sentences.en: As always, there’s much more in the full podcast, which drops every Monday, so listen to and follow us on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Pandora, or wherever you find podcasts.
sentences.zh: 与往常一样，每周一发布的完整播客中有更多内容，因此请在 Apple 播客、Spotify、Pandora 或任何您能找到播客的地方收听并关注我们。

blocks.html_en: You’ve been writing about a phenomenon you call AI populism. How would you define that? What is AI populism?

sentences.en: You’ve been writing about a phenomenon you call AI populism.
sentences.zh: 您一直在写一种被称为人工智能民粹主义的现象。

sentences.en: How would you define that?
sentences.zh: 你会如何定义它？

sentences.en: What is AI populism?
sentences.zh: 什么是人工智能民粹主义？

blocks.html_en: I define AI populism as a worldview where AI is not seen as an ordinary technology, but specifically as an elite political project to be resisted. I came to the term while thinking about the AI backlash and the reasons people are increasingly anti-AI — whether that’s LLM slop, whether that’s Waymos in their city, whether that’s a new data center project. One thing that occurred to me was that a lot of times the debate wasn’t about whether ChatGPT is useful to me, or whether Waymos are safer than a human driver. The debate was actually something more like: There are these big corporations and unaccountable billionaires who are coming into my city, coming into my life, and changing it without any democratic input.

sentences.en: I define AI populism as a worldview where AI is not seen as an ordinary technology, but specifically as an elite political project to be resisted.
sentences.zh: 我将人工智能民粹主义定义为一种世界观，其中人工智能不被视为一种普通技术，而是特别被视为一个需要抵制的精英政治项目。

sentences.en: I came to the term while thinking about the AI backlash and the reasons people are increasingly anti-AI — whether that’s LLM slop, whether that’s Waymos in their city, whether that’s a new data center project.
sentences.zh: 我在思考人工智能的强烈反对以及人们越来越反人工智能的原因时想到了这个词——无论是法学硕士的失败，还是他们所在城市的 Waymos，是否是一个新的数据中心项目。

sentences.en: One thing that occurred to me was that a lot of times the debate wasn’t about whether ChatGPT is useful to me, or whether Waymos are safer than a human driver.
sentences.zh: 我想到的一件事是，很多时候争论并不是关于 ChatGPT 对我是否有用，或者 Waymos 是否比人类司机更安全。

sentences.en: The debate was actually something more like: There are these big corporations and unaccountable billionaires who are coming into my city, coming into my life, and changing it without any democratic input.
sentences.zh: 这场辩论实际上更像是：这些大公司和不负责任的亿万富翁正在进入我的城市，进入我的生活，并在没有任何民主投入的情况下改变它。

blocks.html_en: When I talk to people who are opposing AI in various ways, they seem more concerned with this concentration-of-power, anti-elite dimension — which is where I take the word “populism” — rather than classic AI safety concerns, which are more about the technical characteristics that might introduce risk.

sentences.en: When I talk to people who are opposing AI in various ways, they seem more concerned with this concentration-of-power, anti-elite dimension — which is where I take the word “populism” — rather than classic AI safety concerns, which are more about the technical characteristics that might introduce risk.
sentences.zh: 当我与以各种方式反对人工智能的人交谈时，他们似乎更关心这种权力集中、反精英的维度——这就是我使用“民粹主义”这个词的地方——而不是经典的人工智能安全问题，后者更多地关注可能带来风险的技术特征。

blocks.html_en: You wrote a piece that touched on some of this but went to a darker place — “AI populism’s warning shots” — and you wrote about actual shots. Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, was targeted by a Molotov cocktail and a shooting within the span of a couple of days. There was an Indiana councilman who voted for a data center and woke up to gunshots at his home and a note reading “no data centers.” Why do you think of those incidents of violence as warning shots of something to come?

sentences.en: You wrote a piece that touched on some of this but went to a darker place — “AI populism’s warning shots” — and you wrote about actual shots.
sentences.zh: 你写了一篇文章，触及了其中的一些内容，但去了一个更黑暗的地方——“人工智能民粹主义的警告镜头”——你写了真实的镜头。

sentences.en: Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, was targeted by a Molotov cocktail and a shooting within the span of a couple of days.
sentences.zh: OpenAI 首席执行官萨姆·奥尔特曼 (Sam Altman) 在几天之内成为燃烧弹和枪击事件的目标。

sentences.en: There was an Indiana councilman who voted for a data center and woke up to gunshots at his home and a note reading “no data centers.”
sentences.zh: 有一位印第安纳州议员投票赞成建立数据中心，醒来后发现家中响起枪声，并看到一张写着“禁止数据中心”的字条。

sentences.en: Why do you think of those incidents of violence as warning shots of something to come?
sentences.zh: 为什么您认为这些暴力事件是对即将发生的事情的警告？

blocks.html_en: It was pretty scary. I’m no Sam Altman fanboy, but it’s terrifying that assassination attempts are showing up in response to people’s worries about AI. One factor is that we’ve been seeing a rising wave of political violence and support for political violence in the US, especially among young people, over the past few years — the UnitedHealthcare CEO shooting, the Charlie Kirk shooting. Increasingly, a lot of disaffected, maybe nihilistic young people are turning toward political violence as a way to express political beliefs they don’t feel they have other channels for. Or maybe that person is just unwell. But I do expect to see more of it, because my theory of political discontent is that if people feel they have institutional channels to bargain for their rights — if they feel the democratic process is working, or they’re part of a union and believe their union leader will go bargain about how automation shows up in the workplace — they’ll most likely go through those channels.

sentences.en: It was pretty scary.
sentences.zh: 真是太可怕了。

sentences.en: I’m no Sam Altman fanboy, but it’s terrifying that assassination attempts are showing up in response to people’s worries about AI.
sentences.zh: 我不是山姆·奥尔特曼的粉丝，但令人恐惧的是，由于人们对人工智能的担忧而出现了暗杀企图。

sentences.en: One factor is that we’ve been seeing a rising wave of political violence and support for political violence in the US, especially among young people, over the past few years — the UnitedHealthcare CEO shooting, the Charlie Kirk shooting.
sentences.zh: 一个因素是，过去几年，我们看到美国的政治暴力和对政治暴力的支持不断上升，尤其是在年轻人中——联合医疗首席执行官枪击案、查理·柯克枪击案。

sentences.en: Increasingly, a lot of disaffected, maybe nihilistic young people are turning toward political violence as a way to express political beliefs they don’t feel they have other channels for.
sentences.zh: 越来越多的不满甚至虚无主义的年轻人开始转向政治暴力，以此作为表达他们认为没有其他渠道的政治信仰的一种方式。

sentences.en: Or maybe that person is just unwell.
sentences.zh: 或者也许那个人只是身体不舒服。

sentences.en: But I do expect to see more of it, because my theory of political discontent is that if people feel they have institutional channels to bargain for their rights — if they feel the democratic process is working, or they’re part of a union and believe their union leader will go bargain about how automation shows up in the workplace — they’ll most likely go through those channels.
sentences.zh: 但我确实希望看到更多这样的情况，因为我的政治不满理论是，如果人们觉得他们有制度渠道来争取自己的权利——如果他们觉得民主进程正在发挥作用，或者他们是工会的一部分，并相信工会领导人会就自动化如何出现在工作场所进行讨价还价——他们很可能会通过这些渠道。

blocks.html_en: When it feels like the official channels aren’t working, opposition becomes much more diffuse and volatile. That’s part of why, in creative communities, you’ll see people witch-hunting each other over AI use. I think we’ll see more political violence against people seen as AI leaders, or as supporting AI leaders.

sentences.en: When it feels like the official channels aren’t working, opposition becomes much more diffuse and volatile.
sentences.zh: 当感觉官方渠道不起作用时，反对意见就会变得更加分散和不稳定。

sentences.en: That’s part of why, in creative communities, you’ll see people witch-hunting each other over AI use.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么在创意社区中，你会看到人们因人工智能的使用而互相迫害。

sentences.en: I think we’ll see more political violence against people seen as AI leaders, or as supporting AI leaders.
sentences.zh: 我认为我们会看到更多针对被视为人工智能领导者或支持人工智能领导者的政治暴力。

blocks.html_en: That’s really scary.

sentences.en: That’s really scary.
sentences.zh: 这真的很可怕。

blocks.html_en: Yeah, I’m quite worried about it. But again, my sense is that it comes from a feeling of — what else is there to be done, when you have this level of concentration of wealth and power, and there’s no democratic input right now into how AI is regulated or built?

sentences.en: Yeah, I’m quite worried about it.
sentences.zh: 是的，我很担心。

sentences.en: But again, my sense is that it comes from a feeling of — what else is there to be done, when you have this level of concentration of wealth and power, and there’s no democratic input right now into how AI is regulated or built?
sentences.zh: 但同样，我的感觉是，它来自于一种感觉——当你拥有这种程度的财富和权力集中度，而且目前还没有对人工智能的监管或构建方式进行民主投入时，还有什么可做的呢？

blocks.html_en: It’s like a jump straight from complaining at your community meeting about the data center to an act of violence.

sentences.en: It’s like a jump straight from complaining at your community meeting about the data center to an act of violence.
sentences.zh: 这就像从在社区会议上抱怨数据中心直接跳到暴力行为一样。

blocks.html_en: I was talking to some friends about this. During the 20th century in the US, there was a wave of factory mechanization and automation, but unions were really strong — often when a company said, “We’re going to bring in these machines,” they’d sit down with the factory union leader and say, “Okay, you can bring in the machines, but we’re going to couple that with a wage increase,” or a 35-hour workweek, or earlier retirement. There was a channel to make a deal about how automation would show up in your workplace. That meant people were more likely to accept it as something lifting all boats. I don’t think that’s happening now — most of the industries affected by AI aren’t organized in labor unions, and the democratic channels are questionable at best.

sentences.en: I was talking to some friends about this.
sentences.zh: 我正在和一些朋友谈论这个问题。

sentences.en: During the 20th century in the US, there was a wave of factory mechanization and automation, but unions were really strong — often when a company said, “We’re going to bring in these machines,” they’d sit down with the factory union leader and say, “Okay, you can bring in the machines, but we’re going to couple that with a wage increase,” or a 35-hour workweek, or earlier retirement.
sentences.zh: 20世纪的美国，出现了一波工厂机械化和自动化的浪潮，但工会非常强大——通常当一家公司说，“我们要引进这些机器”时，他们会和工厂工会领导人坐下来说，“好吧，你可以引进机器，但我们要把它与加薪结合起来”，或者每周工作35小时，或者提前退休。

sentences.en: There was a channel to make a deal about how automation would show up in your workplace.
sentences.zh: 有一个渠道可以就自动化如何出现在你的工作场所达成一致。

sentences.en: That meant people were more likely to accept it as something lifting all boats.
sentences.zh: 这意味着人们更有可能接受它，认为它是举足轻重的东西。

sentences.en: I don’t think that’s happening now — most of the industries affected by AI aren’t organized in labor unions, and the democratic channels are questionable at best.
sentences.zh: 我认为这种情况现在不会发生——大多数受人工智能影响的行业都没有组织工会，民主渠道充其量也值得怀疑。

blocks.html_en: It’s like when people have agency to be part of the transition, the process goes a lot smoother. Is there a historical analogy for a technological change that didn’t allow for input from the people involved? I’m thinking of the Luddites.

sentences.en: It’s like when people have agency to be part of the transition, the process goes a lot smoother.
sentences.zh: 这就好像当人们有代理参与过渡时，这个过程就会顺利得多。

sentences.en: Is there a historical analogy for a technological change that didn’t allow for input from the people involved?
sentences.zh: 历史上是否存在不允许相关人员参与的技术变革的类比？

sentences.en: I’m thinking of the Luddites.
sentences.zh: 我想到了勒德分子。

blocks.html_en: The Luddites are a good example. When the automated looms were introduced, there was a lot of violence against the looms. The book I’d really recommend here is Carl Benedikt Frey’s The Technology Trap. He’s an Oxford economist who studied a ton of historical examples — in Europe, in China, all over the world — including the Luddites and 20th-century automation. His central question was: In what contexts do workers successfully stop automation, and in what contexts do they allow it to be introduced? How does the political environment, or the balance of power between people and their leaders, change the outcome? He found that when automation was introduced alongside social welfare policies — a higher minimum wage, some form of redistribution — people were much more willing to accept it, which is fairly rational.

sentences.en: The Luddites are a good example.
sentences.zh: 勒德分子就是一个很好的例子。

sentences.en: When the automated looms were introduced, there was a lot of violence against the looms.
sentences.zh: 当自动化织布机问世时，出现了很多针对织布机的暴力行为。

sentences.en: The book I’d really recommend here is Carl Benedikt Frey’s The Technology Trap.
sentences.zh: 我在这里真正推荐的书是 Carl Benedikt Frey 的《技术陷阱》。

sentences.en: He’s an Oxford economist who studied a ton of historical examples — in Europe, in China, all over the world — including the Luddites and 20th-century automation.
sentences.zh: 他是一位牛津大学经济学家，研究了欧洲、中国和世界各地的大量历史例子，包括勒德分子和 20 世纪的自动化。

sentences.en: His central question was: In what contexts do workers successfully stop automation, and in what contexts do they allow it to be introduced?
sentences.zh: 他的核心问题是：在什么情况下工人们能够成功地阻止自动化，以及在什么情况下他们允许引入自动化？

sentences.en: How does the political environment, or the balance of power between people and their leaders, change the outcome?
sentences.zh: 政治环境或人民与其领导人之间的权力平衡如何改变结果？

sentences.en: He found that when automation was introduced alongside social welfare policies — a higher minimum wage, some form of redistribution — people were much more willing to accept it, which is fairly rational.
sentences.zh: 他发现，当自动化与社会福利政策（更高的最低工资、某种形式的再分配）一起引入时，人们更愿意接受它，这是相当理性的。

blocks.html_en: I want to talk about Silicon Valley’s understanding of this backlash more generally. You’re painting a pretty dark picture, and you’re right in the belly of the beast in San Francisco — I’m sure you talk to people involved with AI every day. Is there a moment when it clicked for them that this backlash is real and something they have to take seriously? Or has that happened yet?

sentences.en: I want to talk about Silicon Valley’s understanding of this backlash more generally.
sentences.zh: 我想更广泛地谈谈硅谷对这种强烈抵制的理解。

sentences.en: You’re painting a pretty dark picture, and you’re right in the belly of the beast in San Francisco — I’m sure you talk to people involved with AI every day.
sentences.zh: 你正在描绘一幅非常黑暗的图画，而你就在旧金山的野兽腹中——我相信你每天都会与人工智能相关的人交谈。

sentences.en: Is there a moment when it clicked for them that this backlash is real and something they have to take seriously?
sentences.zh: 有没有某个时刻，他们突然意识到这种强烈反对是真实存在的，并且是他们必须认真对待的事情？

sentences.en: Or has that happened yet?
sentences.zh: 或者这已经发生了吗？

blocks.html_en: I’ve definitely noticed a huge difference, over the past six months, in how seriously people in Silicon Valley take the AI backlash.

sentences.en: I’ve definitely noticed a huge difference, over the past six months, in how seriously people in Silicon Valley take the AI backlash.
sentences.zh: 在过去的六个月里，我确实注意到硅谷人们对人工智能的强烈反对的严肃程度存在巨大差异。

blocks.html_en: Like what?

sentences.en: Like what?
sentences.zh: 比如什么？

blocks.html_en: People just talk about it more. I’d bring up AI populism to people last year, and they’d normally say, “It doesn’t matter — technology always introduces some discontent, people get annoyed but they get used to it, like the internet.” That was the standard reaction last year. Not anymore. I think part of the reason OpenAI and Anthropic have felt pressure to introduce economic policy proposals around job automation is that they’re seeing how worried people are. The data center moratoriums and the broader data center backlash have been surprising and meaningful in getting AI leaders to recognize they have both a messaging problem and an actual problem with the product and the technology they’re introducing.

sentences.en: People just talk about it more.
sentences.zh: 人们只是更多地谈论它。

sentences.en: I’d bring up AI populism to people last year, and they’d normally say, “It doesn’t matter — technology always introduces some discontent, people get annoyed but they get used to it, like the internet.”
sentences.zh: 去年我向人们提出了人工智能民粹主义，他们通常会说，“没关系——技术总是会带来一些不满，人们会感到恼火，但他们会习惯它，就像互联网一样。”

sentences.en: That was the standard reaction last year.
sentences.zh: 这是去年的标准反应。

sentences.en: Not anymore.
sentences.zh: 不再。

sentences.en: I think part of the reason OpenAI and Anthropic have felt pressure to introduce economic policy proposals around job automation is that they’re seeing how worried people are.
sentences.zh: 我认为 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 感受到围绕工作自动化推出经济政策提案的压力，部分原因是他们看到了人们的担忧程度。

sentences.en: The data center moratoriums and the broader data center backlash have been surprising and meaningful in getting AI leaders to recognize they have both a messaging problem and an actual problem with the product and the technology they’re introducing.
sentences.zh: 数据中心的暂停和更广泛的数据中心抵制令人惊讶且有意义，让人工智能领导者认识到他们所推出的产品和技术既存在消息传递问题，也存在实际问题。

blocks.html_en: A lot of the increasing opposition to AI in Washington has caused people to see this too. At first, Trump — as you mentioned — was very pro-AI. He and David Sacks were accelerationists; they wanted AI to go faster and to block attempts at regulation.

sentences.en: A lot of the increasing opposition to AI in Washington has caused people to see this too.
sentences.zh: 华盛顿对人工智能日益强烈的反对也让人们看到了这一点。

sentences.en: At first, Trump — as you mentioned — was very pro-AI.
sentences.zh: 正如你所提到的，特朗普最初非常支持人工智能。

sentences.en: He and David Sacks were accelerationists; they wanted AI to go faster and to block attempts at regulation.
sentences.zh: 他和大卫·萨克斯都是加速主义者。他们希望人工智能走得更快并阻止监管尝试。

blocks.html_en: He was the AI czar.

sentences.en: He was the AI czar.
sentences.zh: 他是人工智能沙皇。

blocks.html_en: “The moratoriums, the regulation fights, even the booing at graduations, the literal assassination attempts — people in Silicon Valley have become much more worried.”

sentences.en: “The moratoriums, the regulation fights, even the booing at graduations, the literal assassination attempts — people in Silicon Valley have become much more worried.”
sentences.zh: “禁令、监管斗争、甚至毕业典礼上的嘘声、字面意义上的暗杀企图——硅谷的人们变得更加担心。”

blocks.html_en: He was the AI czar — he’s no longer the AI czar. But it turned out a lot of other constituencies, both on the left and the right, were pretty opposed. For example, Trump and David Sacks tried to introduce a big federal bill that would preempt all state-level AI regulation — no state could regulate AI for 10 years. They tried to sneak it into a big omnibus bill so no one would notice. But members of Congress realized it was happening, and — whether for kid-safety reasons or frontier-safety reasons — people said, Wait a second, the idea of preventing any state from regulating AI for ten years is crazy. A lot of people organized in Washington to successfully stop that preemption. I think that showed the scale and bipartisanship of a coalition that was very keen to make sure it stayed possible to regulate AI was underestimated. As a result of all this — the moratoriums, the regulation fights, even the booing at graduations, the literal assassination attempts — people in Silicon Valley have become much more worried.

sentences.en: He was the AI czar — he’s no longer the AI czar.
sentences.zh: 他是人工智能沙皇，但他不再是人工智能沙皇。

sentences.en: But it turned out a lot of other constituencies, both on the left and the right, were pretty opposed.
sentences.zh: 但事实证明，许多其他选区，无论是左翼还是右翼，都非常反对。

sentences.en: For example, Trump and David Sacks tried to introduce a big federal bill that would preempt all state-level AI regulation — no state could regulate AI for 10 years.
sentences.zh: 例如，特朗普和大卫·萨克斯试图提出一项大型联邦法案，该法案将优先于所有州级人工智能监管——没有一个州可以在 10 年内监管人工智能。

sentences.en: They tried to sneak it into a big omnibus bill so no one would notice.
sentences.zh: 他们试图将其偷偷放入一张大额综合账单中，这样就不会被人注意到。

sentences.en: But members of Congress realized it was happening, and — whether for kid-safety reasons or frontier-safety reasons — people said, Wait a second, the idea of preventing any state from regulating AI for ten years is crazy.
sentences.zh: 但国会议员意识到这种情况正在发生，而且——无论是出于儿童安全原因还是边境安全原因——人们说，等一下，十年内阻止任何州监管人工智能的想法是疯狂的。

sentences.en: A lot of people organized in Washington to successfully stop that preemption.
sentences.zh: 许多人在华盛顿组织起来，成功阻止了这种先发制人的行动。

sentences.en: I think that showed the scale and bipartisanship of a coalition that was very keen to make sure it stayed possible to regulate AI was underestimated.
sentences.zh: 我认为这表明了一个非常热衷于确保监管人工智能的可能性的联盟的规模和两党合作被低估了。

sentences.en: As a result of all this — the moratoriums, the regulation fights, even the booing at graduations, the literal assassination attempts — people in Silicon Valley have become much more worried.
sentences.zh: 由于所有这一切——禁令、监管之争、甚至毕业典礼上的嘘声、字面意义上的暗杀企图——硅谷的人们变得更加担心。

blocks.html_en: China is our big competitor in the AI race, and it certainly has all the conditions for a populist pushback to AI — youth unemployment is really high, and AI technology is in some ways more advanced at taking over real-world jobs. I was watching a video about fully automated factories and a robot pharmacist. You’re one of the rare American tech reporters who gets to spend time in China, and you wrote a piece that surprised me — you found there wasn’t really a populist backlash to AI there. Why not?

sentences.en: China is our big competitor in the AI race, and it certainly has all the conditions for a populist pushback to AI — youth unemployment is really high, and AI technology is in some ways more advanced at taking over real-world jobs.
sentences.zh: 中国是我们在人工智能竞赛中的主要竞争对手，它当然具备民粹主义抵制人工智能的所有条件——年轻人失业率确实很高，而人工智能技术在某些方面在取代现实世界的工作方面更加先进。

sentences.en: I was watching a video about fully automated factories and a robot pharmacist.
sentences.zh: 我正在观看一个关于全自动工厂和机器人药剂师的视频。

sentences.en: You’re one of the rare American tech reporters who gets to spend time in China, and you wrote a piece that surprised me — you found there wasn’t really a populist backlash to AI there.
sentences.zh: 你是少数能够在中国呆过的美国科技记者之一，你写了一篇令我惊讶的文章——你发现那里并没有民粹主义对人工智能的强烈抵制。

sentences.en: Why not?
sentences.zh: 为什么不呢？

blocks.html_en: I was really interested in this question, and I was finishing my New York Times piece while in China for a few weeks, talking to both AI people and non-AI people. The main reason there’s not a big populist backlash in China is that there isn’t a lot of social unrest or populist backlash against anything — the entire MO of the Chinese government, the No. 1 priority, is domestic social stability. Any whisper of protest gets shut down; that’s why they have such strong speech controls. So one factor is that China doesn’t have much of a culture of resistance in general, whether in workplaces or politically. I’m not saying no one dissents — but it has a cultural effect too, because people don’t see it as useful or as an option. When I ask family members of mine in China about AI, sometimes they’re annoyed about specific things, but fundamentally, the idea of opposing AI is seen as almost unimaginable.

sentences.en: I was really interested in this question, and I was finishing my New York Times piece while in China for a few weeks, talking to both AI people and non-AI people.
sentences.zh: 我对这个问题真的很感兴趣，在中国呆了几周，我正在完成《纽约时报》的文章，与人工智能人士和非人工智能人士交谈。

sentences.en: The main reason there’s not a big populist backlash in China is that there isn’t a lot of social unrest or populist backlash against anything — the entire MO of the Chinese government, the No. 1 priority, is domestic social stability.
sentences.zh: 中国没有出现大的民粹主义反弹的主要原因是，没有太多的社会动荡或民粹主义对任何事情的反弹——中国政府的整个行动，第一要务，是国内社会稳定。

sentences.en: Any whisper of protest gets shut down; that’s why they have such strong speech controls.
sentences.zh: 任何抗议的低语都会被关闭；这就是为什么他们有如此强大的言论控制能力。

sentences.en: So one factor is that China doesn’t have much of a culture of resistance in general, whether in workplaces or politically.
sentences.zh: 因此，一个因素是，中国总体上并没有太多的抵抗文化，无论是在工作场所还​​是在政治上。

sentences.en: I’m not saying no one dissents — but it has a cultural effect too, because people don’t see it as useful or as an option.
sentences.zh: 我并不是说没有人反对——但它也有一种文化影响，因为人们不认为它有用或不是一种选择。

sentences.en: When I ask family members of mine in China about AI, sometimes they’re annoyed about specific things, but fundamentally, the idea of opposing AI is seen as almost unimaginable.
sentences.zh: 当我向中国的家人询问有关人工智能的问题时，有时他们会对某些具体事情感到恼火，但从根本上来说，反对人工智能的想法几乎被认为是不可想象的。

blocks.html_en: The other thing about China is that if you’re middle-aged there, you’ve lived through so many political, economic, and technological revolutions in your lifetime. When I was a little kid visiting Shanghai in the mid-2000s, there were no high-speed trains — now China has some of the best high-speed rail systems in the world. Technology has always gone hand in hand with dramatic economic advancement, with being lifted out of poverty. The modernization process has been aggressive and disruptive, but it’s not something the party has offered opportunities to resist, and it’s something most Chinese people still see as an inevitability that was mostly good for most people — because incomes did increase by dramatic amounts alongside the technological change. So I think people have a similar attitude toward AI: It’s much less about “Can I stop the AI wave?” and more “How can I take advantage of the AI wave to get ahead economically?”

sentences.en: The other thing about China is that if you’re middle-aged there, you’ve lived through so many political, economic, and technological revolutions in your lifetime.
sentences.zh: 中国的另一件事是，如果你在中国人到中年，你一生中经历了如此多的政治、经济和技术革命。

sentences.en: When I was a little kid visiting Shanghai in the mid-2000s, there were no high-speed trains — now China has some of the best high-speed rail systems in the world.
sentences.zh: 当我还是个孩子的时候，2000年代中期访问上海时，还没有高铁——现在中国拥有一些世界上最好的高铁系统。

sentences.en: Technology has always gone hand in hand with dramatic economic advancement, with being lifted out of poverty.
sentences.zh: 技术总是与经济的巨大进步、摆脱贫困齐头并进。

sentences.en: The modernization process has been aggressive and disruptive, but it’s not something the party has offered opportunities to resist, and it’s something most Chinese people still see as an inevitability that was mostly good for most people — because incomes did increase by dramatic amounts alongside the technological change.
sentences.zh: 现代化进程具有侵略性和破坏性，但党并没有提供机会抵制它，大多数中国人仍然认为这是不可避免的，这对大多数人来说基本上是好事——因为随着技术变革，收入确实大幅增加。

sentences.en: and more “How can I take advantage of the AI wave to get ahead economically?”
sentences.zh: 以及更多“我如何利用人工智能浪潮在经济上取得领先？”

blocks.html_en: We were just talking about this deep pessimism about what technology can bring us here in the US. I think a lot of people look around and think: We don’t have a cure for cancer yet, but we’ve sure seen our lives get worse in a lot of ways because of technology, social media, whatever. That pessimism probably fuels the backlash to AI, the skepticism about whether it can ever deliver on its promises. And that experience just isn’t the same in China, or probably much of the rest of the world, where technological progress has been faster and more concrete in people’s lives.

sentences.en: We were just talking about this deep pessimism about what technology can bring us here in the US.
sentences.zh: 我们刚刚谈论了对技术可以给美国带来什么的深深悲观情绪。

sentences.en: I think a lot of people look around and think: We don’t have a cure for cancer yet, but we’ve sure seen our lives get worse in a lot of ways because of technology, social media, whatever.
sentences.zh: 我想很多人环顾四周都会想：我们还没有治愈癌症的方法，但我们确实看到我们的生活在很多方面因为技术、社交媒体等而变得更糟。

sentences.zh: 这种悲观情绪可能加剧了对人工智能的强烈反对，人们怀疑它是否能够兑现其承诺。

sentences.zh: 这种经历在中国或世界其他大部分地区是不一样的，那里的技术进步更快、更具体地融入到人们的生活中。

blocks.html_en: My 90-year-old grandfather said he’d love an elder-care robot to help him do tasks around the house so he doesn’t have to rely on his kids — he wants more freedom and mobility. It’s seen more as a tool to help individual goals. Even with the robot factories or pharmacies — one thing that struck me visiting a robot pharmacy was that the PR people happily said, “Yep, we’re doing these robots because human workers take too many smoke breaks and bathroom breaks and take too long.”

sentences.zh: 我 90 岁的祖父说，他希望有一个老年护理机器人来帮助他做家务，这样他就不必依赖他的孩子——他想要更多的自由和机动性。

sentences.zh: 即使有了机器人工厂或药房，在参观机器人药房时令我印象深刻的一件事是，公关人员高兴地说，“是的，我们正在做这些机器人，因为人类工人需要太多的抽烟时间和上厕所时间，而且时间太长。”

blocks.html_en: You’d never say that in the US, but they’re probably thinking the same thing — they just don’t say it. The other thing they mentioned is that this lets the pharmacy operate 24/7, because a lot of people need medications in the middle of the night and want to order via the DoorDash equivalent. There was actually a labor shortage before — Chinese workers weren’t willing to work night shifts — so these pharmacies are offering real consumer surplus. A significant percentage of orders come in overnight, when no other pharmacy is open. And with the factories, part of the issue is that Chinese workers, especially young people, don’t want to do factory work anymore.

blocks.html_en: That anecdote gets at the promise and peril of AI, and the role of the backlash movement — which I’m still wrestling with how I feel about. On the one hand, I want to live in a world where cancer gets cured, where we live in an era of abundance, where things are cheap and easy to make because factories can run all the time with machine workers who don’t require anything — I want the future we were promised, of flying cars and everything working well.

blocks.html_en: To close out the conversation — let’s come back to the United States. AI populism is brewing as a political force. We’ve seen it show up in a couple of races so far, but it’s early. We’ve got the midterms, then the presidential election. How do you think it’s going to affect American politics this November, and in 2028?

blocks.html_en: My sense is that this November, it’s going to be more about state and local races where AI really shows up. I’m going to spend some time in Michigan and Wisconsin this summer touring some of the data center sites facing the most opposition — those states also have contested governor and Senate races where AI and data centers have become a core issue, so I’m interested to learn more there. I think the 2028 presidential primary and election is really where I expect AI to become a centerpiece of the conversation — especially if we start to see some of the employment impacts people are expecting. As soon as we see something like a 2 percent rise in unemployment, if that happens, I think people will be very upset, and we should expect a ton of focus on the issue.

blocks.html_en: The other thing I’ll note is political opportunism — you’re already seeing a bit of this, where politicians are likely to raise the salience of AI above where people might ordinarily care about it, because it’s become a convenient boogeyman. I think a lot of politicians who are being clever about this are going to move AI to the center of the conversation, raising its salience to manufacture urgency for proposals they’re already excited about. content_html_zh:
人工智能应该让我们的生活变得更美好。 相反，它让我们许多人感到害怕和愤怒。 社区正在抗议全国范围内建设新的数据中心（为人工智能建设提供动力的 IT 设备仓库），但他们越来越多地获胜。 民意调查显示，大多数美国人认为人工智能发展得太快。

那么公众对人工智能的看法为何如此迅速地消退呢？ 在旧金山报道该行业的 Jasmine Sun 认为，这种强烈反对情绪较少将人工智能视为一项技术，而更多地将其视为一项政治项目。 “争论的焦点不是 ChatGPT 对我有用吗？” Sun 在录制 Vox 的《灰色地带》时告诉我的。 “这场辩论实际上更像是，这些大公司和不负责任的亿万富翁……进入我的城市，进入我的生活并在没有任何民主投入的情况下改变它？”

我代替 Sean Illing 与 Sun 讨论了“人工智能民粹主义”的兴起、与工业革命的相似之处，以及这种强烈反对可能如何影响 2028 年总统选举。

与往常一样，每周一发布的完整播客中有更多内容，因此请在 Apple 播客、Spotify、Pandora 或任何您能找到播客的地方收听并关注我们。

您一直在写一种被称为人工智能民粹主义的现象。 你会如何定义它？ 什么是人工智能民粹主义？

我将人工智能民粹主义定义为一种世界观，其中人工智能不被视为一种普通技术，而是特别被视为一个需要抵制的精英政治项目。 我在思考人工智能的强烈反对以及人们越来越反人工智能的原因时想到了这个词——无论是法学硕士的失败，还是他们所在城市的 Waymos，是否是一个新的数据中心项目。 我想到的一件事是，很多时候争论并不是关于 ChatGPT 对我是否有用，或者 Waymos 是否比人类司机更安全。 这场辩论实际上更像是：这些大公司和不负责任的亿万富翁正在进入我的城市，进入我的生活，并在没有任何民主投入的情况下改变它。

当我与以各种方式反对人工智能的人交谈时，他们似乎更关心这种权力集中、反精英的维度——这就是我使用“民粹主义”这个词的地方——而不是经典的人工智能安全问题，后者更多地关注可能带来风险的技术特征。

你写了一篇文章，触及了其中的一些内容，但去了一个更黑暗的地方——“人工智能民粹主义的警告镜头”——你写了真实的镜头。 OpenAI 首席执行官萨姆·奥尔特曼 (Sam Altman) 在几天之内成为燃烧弹和枪击事件的目标。 有一位印第安纳州议员投票赞成建立数据中心，醒来后发现家中响起枪声，并看到一张写着“禁止数据中心”的字条。 为什么您认为这些暴力事件是对即将发生的事情的警告？

真是太可怕了。 我不是山姆·奥尔特曼的粉丝，但令人恐惧的是，由于人们对人工智能的担忧而出现了暗杀企图。 一个因素是，过去几年，我们看到美国的政治暴力和对政治暴力的支持不断上升，尤其是在年轻人中——联合医疗首席执行官枪击案、查理·柯克枪击案。 越来越多的不满甚至虚无主义的年轻人开始转向政治暴力，以此作为表达他们认为没有其他渠道的政治信仰的一种方式。 或者也许那个人只是身体不舒服。 但我确实希望看到更多这样的情况，因为我的政治不满理论是，如果人们觉得他们有制度渠道来争取自己的权利——如果他们觉得民主进程正在发挥作用，或者他们是工会的一部分，并相信工会领导人会就自动化如何出现在工作场所进行讨价还价——他们很可能会通过这些渠道。

当感觉官方渠道不起作用时，反对意见就会变得更加分散和不稳定。 这就是为什么在创意社区中，你会看到人们因人工智能的使用而互相迫害。 我认为我们会看到更多针对被视为人工智能领导者或支持人工智能领导者的政治暴力。

这真的很可怕。

是的，我很担心。 但同样，我的感觉是，它来自于一种感觉——当你拥有这种程度的财富和权力集中度，而且目前还没有对人工智能的监管或构建方式进行民主投入时，还有什么可做的呢？

这就像从在社区会议上抱怨数据中心直接跳到暴力行为一样。

我正在和一些朋友谈论这个问题。 20世纪的美国，出现了一波工厂机械化和自动化的浪潮，但工会非常强大——通常当一家公司说，“我们要引进这些机器”时，他们会和工厂工会领导人坐下来说，“好吧，你可以引进机器，但我们要把它与加薪结合起来”，或者每周工作35小时，或者提前退休。 有一个渠道可以就自动化如何出现在你的工作场所达成一致。 这意味着人们更有可能接受它，认为它是举足轻重的东西。 我认为这种情况现在不会发生——大多数受人工智能影响的行业都没有组织工会，民主渠道充其量也值得怀疑。

这就好像当人们有代理参与过渡时，这个过程就会顺利得多。 历史上是否存在不允许相关人员参与的技术变革的类比？ 我想到了勒德分子。

勒德分子就是一个很好的例子。 当自动化织布机问世时，出现了很多针对织布机的暴力行为。 我在这里真正推荐的书是 Carl Benedikt Frey 的《技术陷阱》。 他是一位牛津大学经济学家，研究了欧洲、中国和世界各地的大量历史例子，包括勒德分子和 20 世纪的自动化。 他的核心问题是：在什么情况下工人们能够成功地阻止自动化，以及在什么情况下他们允许引入自动化？ 政治环境或人民与其领导人之间的权力平衡如何改变结果？ 他发现，当自动化与社会福利政策（更高的最低工资、某种形式的再分配）一起引入时，人们更愿意接受它，这是相当理性的。

我想更广泛地谈谈硅谷对这种强烈抵制的理解。 你正在描绘一幅非常黑暗的图画，而你就在旧金山的野兽腹中——我相信你每天都会与人工智能相关的人交谈。 有没有某个时刻，他们突然意识到这种强烈反对是真实存在的，并且是他们必须认真对待的事情？ 或者这已经发生了吗？

在过去的六个月里，我确实注意到硅谷人们对人工智能的强烈反对的严肃程度存在巨大差异。

比如什么？

人们只是更多地谈论它。 去年我向人们提出了人工智能民粹主义，他们通常会说，“没关系——技术总是会带来一些不满，人们会感到恼火，但他们会习惯它，就像互联网一样。” 这是去年的标准反应。 不再。 我认为 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 感受到围绕工作自动化推出经济政策提案的压力，部分原因是他们看到了人们的担忧程度。 数据中心的暂停和更广泛的数据中心抵制令人惊讶且有意义，让人工智能领导者认识到他们所推出的产品和技术既存在消息传递问题，也存在实际问题。

华盛顿对人工智能日益强烈的反对也让人们看到了这一点。 正如你所提到的，特朗普最初非常支持人工智能。 他和大卫·萨克斯都是加速主义者。他们希望人工智能走得更快并阻止监管尝试。

他是人工智能沙皇。

“禁令、监管斗争、甚至毕业典礼上的嘘声、字面意义上的暗杀企图——硅谷的人们变得更加担心。”

他是人工智能沙皇，但他不再是人工智能沙皇。 但事实证明，许多其他选区，无论是左翼还是右翼，都非常反对。 例如，特朗普和大卫·萨克斯试图提出一项大型联邦法案，该法案将优先于所有州级人工智能监管——没有一个州可以在 10 年内监管人工智能。 他们试图将其偷偷放入一张大额综合账单中，这样就不会被人注意到。 但国会议员意识到这种情况正在发生，而且——无论是出于儿童安全原因还是边境安全原因——人们说，等一下，十年内阻止任何州监管人工智能的想法是疯狂的。 许多人在华盛顿组织起来，成功阻止了这种先发制人的行动。 我认为这表明了一个非常热衷于确保监管人工智能的可能性的联盟的规模和两党合作被低估了。 由于所有这一切——禁令、监管之争、甚至毕业典礼上的嘘声、字面意义上的暗杀企图——硅谷的人们变得更加担心。

中国是我们在人工智能竞赛中的主要竞争对手，它当然具备民粹主义抵制人工智能的所有条件——年轻人失业率确实很高，而人工智能技术在某些方面在取代现实世界的工作方面更加先进。 我正在观看一个关于全自动工厂和机器人药剂师的视频。 你是少数能够在中国呆过的美国科技记者之一，你写了一篇令我惊讶的文章——你发现那里并没有民粹主义对人工智能的强烈抵制。 为什么不呢？

我对这个问题真的很感兴趣，在中国呆了几周，我正在完成《纽约时报》的文章，与人工智能人士和非人工智能人士交谈。 中国没有出现大的民粹主义反弹的主要原因是，没有太多的社会动荡或民粹主义对任何事情的反弹——中国政府的整个行动，第一要务，是国内社会稳定。 任何抗议的低语都会被关闭；这就是为什么他们有如此强大的言论控制能力。 因此，一个因素是，中国总体上并没有太多的抵抗文化，无论是在工作场所还​​是在政治上。 我并不是说没有人反对——但它也有一种文化影响，因为人们不认为它有用或不是一种选择。 当我向中国的家人询问有关人工智能的问题时，有时他们会对某些具体事情感到恼火，但从根本上来说，反对人工智能的想法几乎被认为是不可想象的。

中国的另一件事是，如果你在中国人到中年，你一生中经历了如此多的政治、经济和技术革命。 当我还是个孩子的时候，2000年代中期访问上海时，还没有高铁——现在中国拥有一些世界上最好的高铁系统。 技术总是与经济的巨大进步、摆脱贫困齐头并进。 现代化进程具有侵略性和破坏性，但党并没有提供机会抵制它，大多数中国人仍然认为这是不可避免的，这对大多数人来说基本上是好事——因为随着技术变革，收入确实大幅增加。 所以我认为人们对人工智能也有类似的态度：更少关注“我能阻止人工智能浪潮吗？” 以及更多“我如何利用人工智能浪潮在经济上取得领先？”

我们刚刚谈论了对技术可以给美国带来什么的深深悲观情绪。 我想很多人环顾四周都会想：我们还没有治愈癌症的方法，但我们确实看到我们的生活在很多方面因为技术、社交媒体等而变得更糟。 这种悲观情绪可能加剧了对人工智能的强烈反对，人们怀疑它是否能够兑现其承诺。 这种经历在中国或世界其他大部分地区是不一样的，那里的技术进步更快、更具体地融入到人们的生活中。

我 90 岁的祖父说，他希望有一个老年护理机器人来帮助他做家务，这样他就不必依赖他的孩子——他想要更多的自由和机动性。 它更多地被视为帮助实现个人目标的工具。 即使有了机器人工厂或药房，在参观机器人药房时令我印象深刻的一件事是，公关人员高兴地说，“是的，我们正在做这些机器人，因为人类工人需要太多的抽烟时间和上厕所时间，而且时间太长。”

在美国你永远不会这么说，但他们可能也在想同样的事情——他们只是不说出来。 他们提到的另一件事是，这可以让药房 24/7 运营，因为很多人在半夜需要药物，并且希望通过 DoorDash 等价物订购。 之前实际上存在劳动力短缺——中国工人不愿意上夜班——所以这些药店提供了真正的消费者剩余。 很大一部分订单是在一夜之间收到的，当时没有其他药店营业。 对于工厂来说，部分问题是中国工人，尤其是年轻人，不想再在工厂工作了。

“我认为 2028 年总统初选和选举确实是我希望人工智能成为对话焦点的地方。”

这则轶事讲述了人工智能的前景和危险，以及强烈反对运动的作用——我仍在努力思考自己的感受。 一方面，我希望生活在一个癌症得到治愈的世界，我们生活在一个物质丰富的时代，东西既便宜又容易制造，因为工厂可以一直运转，不需要任何东西的机器工人——我想要我们所承诺的未来，飞行汽车和一切运转良好。

但我也不想失去工作，也不想看到人类被愤怒的机器神消灭。 因为我们还不知道会发生什么，所以很难弄清楚我自己对美国这里的阻力的感受——什么是适当的，什么阻碍了我们生活的真正进步。

完全同意。 我喜欢 Waymos——我认为它们更安全，而且我更喜欢更安全的机器人汽车载着我到处走，而不是我开车。 我不是一个好的司机；没有人应该让我开车。 所以我也在努力解决一些同样的事情。

结束对话——让我们回到美国。 人工智能民粹主义正在酝酿成为一股政治力量。 到目前为止，我们已经在几场比赛中看到了它的出现，但现在还为时过早。 我们有中期选举，然后是总统选举。 您认为这将如何影响今年 11 月和 2028 年的美国政治？

我的感觉是，今年 11 月，人工智能真正出现的州和地方比赛将会更多。 今年夏天，我将在密歇根州和威斯康星州花一些时间参观一些最反对的数据中心站点——这些州也进行了州长和参议院竞选，其中人工智能和数据中心已成为核心问题，所以我有兴趣在那里了解更多信息。 我认为 2028 年总统初选和选举确实是我预计人工智能将成为对话焦点的地方——特别是如果我们开始看到人们期望的一些就业影响的话。 一旦我们看到失业率上升 2%，如果这种情况发生，我认为人们会非常沮丧，我们应该期待对这个问题的大量关注。

我要指出的另一件事是政治机会主义——你已经看到了一些这样的情况，政客们可能会将人工智能的重要性提升到人们通常关心的水平之上，因为它已经成为一个方便的恶魔。 它的民意调查如此糟糕，人们如此反人工智能和反数据中心，人工智能亿万富翁如此没有同情心，以至于无论你的政策计划是什么，人工智能都是推动它的一个很好的理由。 我认为许多在这方面很聪明的政治家会将人工智能转移到对话的中心，提高其重要性，为他们已经兴奋的提案制造紧迫感。 这绝对是我在 2028 年关注的事情。
